headline: CATTLE HOLD $251 AS MARKETS MARK TIME
subheadline: Live cattle found buyers just above yesterday's breakdown level while grains drifted lower in thin trade.
lead: Live cattle closed $251.95, holding the line one day after breaking Monday's $252 support. The bounce is thin but it's there — feeders dropped harder at $366.98, down 1.4%, suggesting the correction isn't finished but maybe pausing. Yesterday's breakdown call is still live: next real support sits at $245, and today's action doesn't change that math.
the_takeaway: Cattle bounce is thin — breakdown story still unfolding toward $245.
teaser: Cattle found buyers above $251 but the breakdown story isn't over.

one_number.context: Feeders dropped harder than live cattle today, typically a sign the correction has more room to run. When feeders lead lower, it's the market pricing weaker demand for replacement cattle — never a bullish signal for the complex.

yesterdays_call.summary: Lock remaining cattle above $250 as next support wasn't expected until $245.
yesterdays_call.note: Today's $251.95 hold keeps the call live but feeder weakness suggests more downside ahead.

sections.title: Cattle Find Thin Support
sections.body: Live cattle closed $251.95, up from yesterday's $250.70 breakdown but not by much. The bounce found buyers just above the $250 line we flagged, but feeders dropped 1.4% to $366.98 — when feeders lead lower, it's the market pricing weaker demand ahead. Open interest in live cattle dropped another 2,100 contracts, suggesting position liquidation continues. The chart wants to test $245 support and today's thin bounce doesn't argue otherwise.
sections.bottom_line: Bounce is real but thin — feeder weakness keeps breakdown thesis alive.
sections.vs_yesterday: Cattle: thin bounce above $250 keeps breakdown call pending.

sections.title: Grains Drift Quietly Lower
sections.body: Corn closed $4.84, down a penny and a half in the kind of drift that says nothing new. Soybeans eased to $12.19, off 4¾ cents, while wheat led the slide at $6.38, down 8¼ cents. Chicago wheat's 1.3% drop was the only move worth mentioning in a session where managed money stayed on the sidelines. The grain complex is coiled in a 10-day range and today's action doesn't break it either direction.
sections.bottom_line: Wheat led lower but grains still range-bound waiting for weather catalyst.

spread_to_watch.label: Live cattle / feeder cattle ratio
spread_to_watch.commentary: When feeders drop harder than live cattle, the ratio narrows and it's telling you something: weaker demand for replacement cattle typically leads weaker cash prices by 2-3 weeks. Today's 1.4% feeder drop versus 0.4% live cattle decline fits the breakdown pattern perfectly.

basis.headline: Eastern corn basis steady as ethanol maintenance wraps up.
basis.body: Eastern Belt corn basis is holding firm as ethanol plants come back online from spring maintenance schedules. Basis is talking about local tightness that the futures board isn't fully pricing. Western Belt staying softer, consistent with the seasonal flow toward the Gulf. Nothing dramatic, just the typical spring basis pattern working as expected.

weekly_thread.question: Will the cattle complex find support at $250 or continue the breakdown through key technical levels?
weekly_thread.status_text: Live cattle bounced to $251.95 but feeders dropped 1.4%, suggesting the breakdown is pausing, not finished. The $250 floor held but barely.

the_more_you_know.title: The Feeder Leading Indicator: Why $366.98 Matters More
the_more_you_know.body: Today's 1.4% drop in feeders to $366.98 tells a different story than live cattle's thin bounce. Feeder cattle are the forward market for live cattle — when feeders lead lower, it's cattlemen pricing weaker demand for replacement animals 6-8 months out. The feeder-to-live ratio narrowed to 0.687 today, and historically when this ratio drops below 0.68, live cattle follow within 2-3 weeks. The breakdown in cattle isn't just about today's technicals; it's about forward demand the feeder market is already pricing.

watch_list.desc: USDA Crop Progress: corn planting above 50% confirms Belt is on schedule.
Weekly export sales: soybeans under 400K MT keeps chart in charge.
Cattle: $248 break would target the $245 support zone directly.

daily_quote.text: If we throw nature out the window, she comes back in the door with a pitchfork.

source_summary: CME futures, USDA reports, commercial basis indicators
